slides.description: The slide is titled "AI Screenplays" in large, bold blue text. Below the title is an illustration of a luxurious, wood-paneled office. A large window overlooks a sunset landscape featuring the 'HOLLYWOOD' sign on a distant hill. A man with grey hair, wearing a suit and red tie, stands behind a large wooden desk, gesturing with his right hand. To his left, on the desk, is a cardboard box robot labeled "A.W.E.S.O.M.-O" with two large black circular eyes and a small rectangular mouth. On the left side of the desk, there are two golden Oscar-like statues, a golden Emmy-like statue, and a small red and gold trophy of a cartoon character with "CARTMAN" written on its base. A black desk phone is also on this side. In the center of the desk, in front of the man, are a brown journal, a document titled "SCRIPTS" in upside-down text, and a beige landline phone. To the right of the man, a globe rests on a stand next to a wooden cabinet. On the cabinet, a fish tank contains a green alien character. Above the cabinet, a framed picture on the wall displays the text "AWESOME-O" with two black circles for the O's, and the word "LAME" stamped in a red rectangle below it. A safe is visible next to the fish tank. On the desk in front of the man, a framed photograph shows a group of animated characters, including the man from the scene.

The slide is titled "Generating Long Documents" in large, bold blue text. Below the title are four bullet points. The first bullet states, "AI can be used to generate long, coherent text documents." The second bullet provides examples: "Books, reports, screenplays." This directly relates to the previous slide's title "AI Screenplays." The third bullet explains, "Today we will build an agentic flow to generate screenplays for movies," reinforcing the theme of AI creating movie scripts introduced visually on the prior slide. The fourth bullet broadens the scope, stating, "This flow architecture can be generalized to other long text documents."

The slide is titled "Challenge: "One-Shot" Long-Form" in large, bold blue text. This title introduces a specific difficulty in the generation of long documents by AI, building on the theme from the previous slide. Below the title are two main bullet points. The first bullet states, "It is difficult for an AI to generate a long coherent document in one-shot," directly defining the "one-shot" challenge. The second bullet elaborates on this difficulty, stating, "Local vs global – the AI is good at writing small parts, but cannot maintain coherence across whole document." This bullet has two sub-points: the first, "– Becomes “lost in the middle”," describes a common issue, and the second, "– Context drift – deep into the document, the AI can hallucinate or lose focus of what to write," explains another problem related to maintaining narrative or informational consistency over extended text.

The slide is titled "Movie Screenplays" in large, bold blue text, continuing the focus on AI-generated screenplays from previous slides. The slide presents two main bullet points detailing elements and structure relevant to screenplays. The first main bullet point states, "Screenplays have many different elements the AI needs to capture." This bullet has three sub-points: "– Story shape", "– Goals of “sequences” in the movie", and "– Scene action and dialogue". The second main bullet point is "Hierarchical structure of a screenplay," which elaborates on how screenplays are organized, potentially addressing the "one-shot" challenge discussed in the previous slide. This bullet also has four sub-points: "– Premise – basic elements of the movie", "– Story arc – high level plot structure", "– Sequences – more detailed plot", and "– Scenes – even more detailed plot".

The slide is titled "Screenplay Flow" in large, bold blue text. Below the title is a flowchart diagram illustrating the hierarchical structure of a screenplay, building on the concepts introduced on the previous slide. The flow starts with a blue rectangular box labeled "Premise" with the text "premise.json" below it. An arrow points from "Premise" to a purple rectangular box labeled "Arc" with "arc.json" below it. From "Arc," two arrows branch out: one points to an orange rectangular box labeled "Sequences" with "sequence.json," and the other points to a green rectangular box labeled "Scenes" with "scenes.json." Each of the four colored boxes also contains a small icon of a cardboard box robot, similar to the "A.W.E.S.O.M.-O" robot from slide 1, suggesting an AI agent is involved in each step of this generation process. This diagram visually represents the agentic flow to generate screenplays, using the hierarchical elements of Premise, Arc, Sequences, and Scenes that were defined on slide 4, and indicating they produce JSON output at each stage.

The slide is titled "Premise Agent" in large, bold blue text, indicating the first stage of the screenplay generation process introduced on slide 5. In the top right corner, there is a small image of a cardboard box robot, similar to the "A.W.E.S.O.M.-O" robot from slide 1 and the icons used in the flowchart on slide 5, visually representing the AI agent. Below the title are four bullet points. The first bullet describes the Premise Agent as "A chat agent that discusses with you what your movie is about." A sub-bullet specifies the elements discussed: "Title, logline (like an executive summary), characters, genre, central conflict," which elaborates on the 'Premise – basic elements of the movie' from slide 4. The second bullet states, "You can ask the agent to give ideas for the plot if you are stuck." The third bullet explains, "This chat can take a few minutes even if you have a general sense about your movie (takes time to specify all the major elements of the movie)." The final bullet point states, "This agent then creates a file premise.json," which aligns with the 'premise.json' output shown for the 'Premise' stage in the "Screenplay Flow" diagram on slide 5.

The slide is titled "Premise.json" in large, bold blue text, directly referencing the output file from the 'Premise Agent' discussed on slide 6 and the 'Premise' stage of the 'Screenplay Flow' on slide 5. Below the title is a dark-themed code block displaying a JSON object. This JSON object represents the content of the `premise.json` file, detailing the basic elements of a movie. It includes a "project_id" as "shadow_of_the_dark_world_20260402_025456" and a "title" as "Shadow of the Dark World," which are elements discussed by the Premise Agent. The "writers" field is set to "TBD," and "source_script" is null. "format_notes" states, "Awesome-O generated this from a CLI chat," linking to the 'A.W.E.S.O.M.-O' robot from slide 1 and the chat agent description from slide 6. The "logline" provides a summary: "A seemingly ordinary young man, Link, is thrust into a perilous quest after his uncle is gravely wounded rescuing Princess Zelda. He must gather legendary artifacts, wield the fabled Master Sword, and confront a malevolent wizard to prevent the dark lord Ganon's release and the conquest of their world," which is another element discussed by the Premise Agent. The "genre" is an array listing "fantasy," "action," and "adventure." Finally, "themes" is an array including "hero's journey," "destiny," "courage in the face of overwhelming evil," "good versus evil," and "sacrifice." These elements correspond to the 'title, logline, characters, genre, central conflict' mentioned for the Premise Agent on slide 6, and the 'Premise – basic elements of the movie' from slide 4.

The slide is titled "Premise.json" in large, bold blue text, indicating a continuation of the JSON output from the 'Premise Agent' discussed on slide 6. Below the title is a dark-themed code block displaying more content of the `premise.json` file. This segment details the 'central_conflict' as "Link's race against time to understand and embrace his role as a hero, stopping the sorcerer Aghanim from sacrificing Princess Zelda and six other maidens, a ritual that would unleash the ancient evil Ganon from the corrupted Dark World upon the Light World." It also defines the 'setting', which includes 'primary_time' as "High fantasy era, contemporary-feel for characters" and 'primary_places' as an array listing "Hyrule Castle," "Kakariko Village," "Eastern Palace," "Lost Woods," and "Dark World." Furthermore, it introduces 'protagonists' as an array containing an object for "Link," describing his 'name', 'role' as an "Unassuming hero, destined to wield the Master Sword," and his 'want' to "To save his uncle, rescue Zelda, and ultimately prevent Aghanim and Ganon from destroying Hyrule." These fields provide further elaboration on the basic elements of the movie, such as the central conflict, characters, and setting, which were initiated on slide 7 and align with the 'Premise – basic elements of the movie' from slide 4.

The slide is titled "Premise.json" in large, bold blue text, continuing to display the JSON output file from the 'Premise Agent' and 'Premise' stage of the screenplay flow. Below the title is a dark-themed code block showing more content of the `premise.json` file. This segment continues the 'protagonists' array, specifically showing the object for 'Link' with his 'name', 'role' as an "Unassuming hero, destined to wield the Master Sword," and 'want' to "To save his uncle, rescue Zelda, and ultimately prevent Aghanim and Ganon from destroying Hyrule." Following this, it introduces a 'key_antagonist' object, detailing 'Aghanim' with his 'role' as an "Evil sorcerer, Ganon's pawn" and 'want' "To seal the seven maidens, including Princess Zelda, to open a portal to the Dark World and release Ganon." It also introduces a 'key_ally' object, defining 'Sahasrahla' with his 'role' as "Village elder, wise man, guide" and 'want' "To guide Link to acquire the Master Sword and stop Aghanim's plans to release Ganon." Finally, the JSON block includes 'stakes' as "The total conquest of the Light World by the dark lord Ganon, resulting in the enslavement or destruction of its inhabitants." This slide elaborates further on the characters, their motivations, and the overarching stakes, all part of the basic elements of the movie discussed by the Premise Agent and started on slides 7 and 8.

The slide is titled "Arc Agent" in large, bold blue text, indicating the second stage of the screenplay generation process introduced on slide 5. In the top right corner, there is a small image of the cardboard box robot, similar to the "A.W.E.S.O.M.-O" robot from slide 1 and the icons used in the flowchart on slide 5, visually representing this AI agent. Below the title are four bullet points. The first bullet describes the Arc Agent as "A chat agent that discusses your movie story arc." A sub-bullet clarifies, "Arc consists of multiple acts," which aligns with the 'Story arc – high level plot structure' from slide 4. Another sub-bullet specifies, "Agent takes premise.json as input for context," directly linking to the output of the Premise Agent detailed on slides 7-9. The second bullet explains the interaction model: "You tell the agent what happens in each act, then ask it to draft the act, one at a time." The third bullet reiterates, "Premise file gives it context for the story arc." The final bullet point states, "This agent then creates a file arc.json," which corresponds to the 'arc.json' output shown for the 'Arc' stage in the "Screenplay Flow" diagram on slide 5.

The slide is titled "Arc.json" in large, bold blue text. Below the title is a dark-themed code block displaying a JSON object, representing the content of the `arc.json` file. This file is the output of the Arc Agent discussed on slide 10 and corresponds to the 'Arc' stage in the "Screenplay Flow" from slide 5.

The JSON object details 'act: 3' of the movie's story arc. It includes a "label" as "Battle Aghanim and the Dark World" and an "approx_pages_note" indicating this act, encompassing the climax and a major cliffhanger, will be around 20-30 pages of a 120-page screenplay. The "dramatic_question" poses, "Can Link defeat Aghanim, save Zelda and the other maidens, and prevent Ganon from conquering their world, even when cast into the heart of darkness?", directly relating to the central conflict and stakes established in the `premise.json` on slides 8-9.

A "summary" provides a detailed narrative of the act, describing Link finding the Master Sword, Zelda's capture, Aghanim's ritual to release Ganon, Link's battles, and Sahasrahla's guidance, all consistent with the movie's premise (slides 7-9). The "turning_point_end" is described as "Aghanim banishes Link to the corrupted Dark World, setting the stage for a new, desperate quest as the ultimate evil, Ganon, looms large."

An array of "must_hit_beats" outlines key plot points within Act 3. These beats include Sahasrahla guiding Link to the Master Sword in the Lost Woods, Link activating and pulling the sword from the stone, Zelda telepathically contacting Link after being captured, Link's return to Hyrule Castle to battle enchanted soldiers, his confrontation with Aghanim who zaps Zelda away after gathering seven maidens, Aghanim revealing his plan to unleash Ganon, Link battling and wounding Aghanim with the Master Sword, Aghanim banishing Link to the Dark World, Link landing on the Great Pyramid, and Sahasrahla instructing Link to rescue the seven maidens to break the barrier to Ganon's tower. The final field, "midpoint_note", is null.

The slide is titled "Sequence Agent" in large, bold blue text, indicating the next stage in the screenplay generation process from the "Screenplay Flow" introduced on slide 5. In the top right corner, there is a small image of the cardboard box robot (A.W.E.S.O.M.-O), visually representing this AI agent. Below the title are four bullet points.
The first bullet describes the Sequence Agent as "An automated agent (no chat) that writes the sequences of the movie for each act," distinguishing it from the previous chat-based agents and aligning with the 'Sequences – more detailed plot' from slide 4.
The second main bullet, "Inputs per sequence," details the information the agent uses. A sub-bullet states, "Premise.json, arc.json – give agent global view of the movie," indicating it uses the output from the Premise Agent (slides 7-9) and the Arc Agent (slide 11) for context. Another sub-bullet specifies, "All previous sequences generated so far – give agent local context for what should happen next," suggesting an iterative process.
The third main bullet, "Primary sequence elements," outlines what the agent focuses on generating for each sequence. It lists three sub-points: "Goal – what is the narrative purpose of this sequence," "Key locations – locations in the sequence," and "Exit beat – how the sequence ends and transitions to next sequence."
The final bullet point states, "This agent then creates a file sequence.json," which corresponds to the 'sequence.json' output shown for the 'Sequences' stage in the "Screenplay Flow" diagram on slide 5.

The slide is titled "Sequence.json" in large, bold blue text, directly referencing the output file from the 'Sequence Agent' discussed on slide 12 and the 'Sequences' stage of the 'Screenplay Flow' on slide 5. Below the title is a dark-themed code block displaying a JSON object, representing part of the content of the `sequence.json` file. This JSON object begins with a "project_id" as "shadow_of_the_dark_world_20260402_025456", consistent with the `premise.json` file. It indicates a "sequence_count" of 8 and a null "note" field. The core of the object is a "sequences" array, of which the first sequence, identified by "id": "SEQ1", is fully displayed. This sequence is for "act": 1 and has a "title" of "Zelda's Urgent Plea." Its "goal" is described as "To establish Link's call to adventure, his initial motivation (saving his uncle and Zelda), and to equip him with his first tools for the perilous journey ahead," aligning with the 'Goal' element mentioned on slide 12. The "emotional_tone" is set to "Urgent, sorrowful, determined." The "key_locations" array includes "Link's Home" and "Hyrule Castle (Outer Walls)," corresponding to the 'Key locations' element from slide 12. Finally, the "exit_beat" states, "Armed with his uncle's sword and shield, Link steels himself, ready to infiltrate the formidable Hyrule Castle and rescue Princess Zelda," which matches the 'Exit beat' element from slide 12. The JSON structure indicates that additional sequences would follow, but they are not visible on this slide.

The slide is titled "Scene Agent" in large, bold blue text, indicating the final stage in the screenplay generation process from the "Screenplay Flow" introduced on slide 5. In the top right corner, there is a small image of the cardboard box robot (A.W.E.S.O.M.-O), visually representing this AI agent. Below the title are four bullet points. The first bullet describes the Scene Agent as "An automated agent (no chat) that writes the scenes of the movie for each sequence," distinguishing it as an automated process like the Sequence Agent on slide 12, and aligning with the 'Scenes – even more detailed plot' from slide 4. The second main bullet, "Inputs per sequence," details the information the agent uses. A sub-bullet states, "Premise.json, arc.json, sequence.json – give agent global view of the movie," indicating it uses the output from the Premise Agent (slides 7-9), the Arc Agent (slide 11), and the Sequence Agent (slide 13) for overall context. Another sub-bullet specifies, "All scenes generated so far in this sequence and the previous sequence – give agent local context for what should happen next," suggesting an iterative generation process that builds on prior scene content. The third main bullet, "Primary scene elements," outlines what the agent focuses on generating for each scene. It lists two sub-points: "Slug line – location of the scene" and "Blocks – series of actions and dialogues in the scene," which provide concrete elements for 'Scene action and dialogue' mentioned on slide 4. The final bullet point states, "This agent then creates a file scenes.json," which directly corresponds to the 'scenes.json' output shown for the 'Scenes' stage in the "Screenplay Flow" diagram on slide 5.

The slide is titled "Scenes.json" in large, bold blue text, directly referencing the output file from the 'Scene Agent' discussed on slide 14 and the 'Scenes' stage of the 'Screenplay Flow' on slide 5. Below the title is a dark-themed code block displaying a JSON object, representing a portion of the content of the `scenes.json` file. The root of the JSON is a "scenes" array, containing an object for the first scene. This scene has an "id" of "S0001", a "slug_id" of "SEQ1-01" indicating it's the first scene of Sequence 1, and a "slugline" as "INT. LINK'S HOME - NIGHT", which corresponds to the 'Slug line' element described for the Scene Agent on slide 14. The "line_start" field is null, and the "sequence_id" is "SEQ1". The core of the scene is presented in a "blocks" array, which aligns with the 'Blocks' element from slide 14, detailing actions and dialogues. The blocks describe Link sleeping in his home, a desperate whisper from ZELDA (V.O.) echoing in his mind saying "Link... help me... I'm trapped in the castle... Aghanim... has me...", Link jolting awake, and then clutching his head as he sees visions of Hyrule Castle, Princess Zelda's terrified face, and the menacing silhouette of a sorcerer. This narrative content directly builds upon the movie's 'Premise' (slides 7-9), 'Arc' (slide 11), and specifically 'Sequence 1: Zelda's Urgent Plea' (slide 13), establishing Link's call to adventure and the central conflict with Aghanim.

The slide is titled "Enhancement Agents" in large, bold blue text. This title introduces a new type of agent in the screenplay generation process, building on the Premise, Arc, Sequence, and Scene Agents from previous slides. Below the title is a bullet point that states, "We can improve the agentic flow by using agents to enhance intermediate outputs." This bullet has three sub-points: "– Write better sequences or scenes," "– Clean up the premise," and "– Make the arc more creative." To the right of the bullet points is an image of a golden cardboard box robot, similar to the 'A.W.E.S.O.M.-O' robot from slide 1, with a label on its chest that reads "ENHANCEMENT AGENT." Below the text and the robot image is a flowchart diagram. This diagram is a modified version of the "Screenplay Flow" seen on slide 5. It shows three of the original rectangular boxes: a blue "Premise" box with "premise.json," a purple "Arc" box with "arc.json," and an orange "Sequences" box with "sequence.json." A green "Scenes" box with "scenes.json" is positioned below the Arc box. Unlike the original flow on slide 5 where arrows directly connected the main agent boxes, this diagram now features small images of the cardboard box robot (similar to the 'A.W.E.S.O.M.-O' and the Enhancement Agent robot) positioned between the boxes. An arrow points from the 'Premise' box to one of these robot images, which then points to the 'Arc' box. Another arrow points from the 'Arc' box to a robot image, which then points to the 'Sequences' box. A separate arrow points from the 'Arc' box to another robot image, which then points to the 'Scenes' box. Each of the main boxes (Premise, Arc, Sequences, Scenes) also contains a small icon of the cardboard box robot, indicating their role as agents. The placement of the robot images between the main stages suggests that these 'Enhancement Agents' can operate on or between the outputs of the primary agents (Premise, Arc, Sequences, Scenes) to refine them, as described in the bullet points.

The slide is titled "Generalizing to Other Documents" in large, bold blue text, directly addressing the concept introduced on slide 2 about generalizing the agentic flow. Below the title is a bullet point that states, "By modifying the agent prompts, this hierarchical flow can be used for other long text documents," reinforcing that the multi-stage AI generation process, like the 'Screenplay Flow' introduced on slide 5, can be adapted.

The main content of the slide is a table with six columns: "Document," "Premise," "Arc," "Sequence," "Scene," and "Artist Rendering." This table illustrates how the hierarchical structure used for screenplays (Premise, Arc, Sequence, Scene) can be applied to different types of documents:

*   For a **Book**: The 'Premise' maps to "Premise," 'Arc' to "Table of contents," 'Sequence' to "Chapter," and 'Scene' to "Sub-chapter."
*   For a **Podcast**: The 'Premise' maps to "Concept," 'Arc' to "Flow, format," 'Sequence' to "Segment," and 'Scene' to "Beat."
*   For a **Song**: The 'Premise' maps to "Premise," 'Arc' to "Structure (verse, hooks, coda)," 'Sequence' to "Verse," and 'Scene' to "Couplet."
*   For a **Lecture**: The 'Premise' maps to "Learning objective," 'Arc' to "Outline," 'Sequence' to "Module," and 'Scene' to "Slide."
*   For a **Business plan**: The 'Premise' maps to "Value prop," 'Arc' to "Outline," 'Sequence' to "Section," and 'Scene' to "Sub-section."

The "Artist Rendering" column for each document type displays a small illustrative image featuring the cardboard box robot, similar to the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O robot from slide 1, depicted in a setting relevant to the document type (e.g., in a library for a book, a recording studio for a podcast, a stage for a song, a lecture hall for a lecture, and an office for a business plan), visually representing the AI agent's role in generating these diverse documents.

The slide is titled "Coding Session" in large, bold blue text. Below the title are three bullet points. The first bullet states, "Write a short screenplay using Awesome-O," referring to the AI robot and the main topic of the presentation. The second bullet states, "Add enhancement agents to the agentic flow," which directly relates to the concept of Enhancement Agents introduced on slide 16. The third bullet states, "Modify Awesome-O for different document types," referencing the generalization of the AI agent for various long text documents discussed on slide 17.

Below the bullet points is a flowchart diagram that is identical to the modified "Screenplay Flow" shown on slide 16, illustrating the inclusion of enhancement agents. The flow starts with a blue rectangular box labeled "Premise" with "premise.json" below it. An arrow points from "Premise" to a small image of a cardboard box robot, which then points to a purple rectangular box labeled "Arc" with "arc.json." From "Arc," two arrows branch out: one points to a cardboard box robot image, which then points to an orange rectangular box labeled "Sequences" with "sequence.json." The other arrow from "Arc" points to a different cardboard box robot image, which then points to a green rectangular box labeled "Scenes" with "scenes.json." Each of the four colored boxes (Premise, Arc, Sequences, Scenes) also contains a small icon of a cardboard box robot, similar to the "A.W.E.S.O.M.-O" robot from slide 1, representing the agent for each stage. The presence of the robot images between the main stages reinforces the idea of enhancement agents operating on or between the outputs of the primary agents.
